postCopy.instagram: Belfast, 1972. A man is dead inside an IRA safehouse — and the only witness swears a ghost pulled the trigger. Someone planned this perfectly. Can you see through the lie?
postCopy.tiktok: A murder mystery game set in a 1972 IRA safehouse — one witness, one impossible story, and a killer hiding behind Belfast folklore. Play today's daily deduction puzzle free. Solve the case before time runs out.

marketing.imagePrompt: Interior of a cramped 1972 Belfast terraced house converted into an IRA safehouse. Low-ceilinged back room lit by a single bare bulb casting amber shadows. Peeling floral wallpaper, a wooden table with a half-empty bottle of Bushmills, a overturned chair, and a dead man slumped against the wall. Mist seeps under the door. A tattered St. Brigid's cross hangs askew above the fireplace. Color palette: deep charcoal, soot black, amber, and damp green. Style reference: gritty 1970s British social realism photography meets Irish Gothic illustration — Roger Deakins low-light cinematography.
marketing.animationPrompt: Begin with a slow push through rain-streaked glass into the safehouse back room. The bare bulb sways gently, casting shifting shadows across the dead man's face. Camera drifts to the St. Brigid's cross on the wall — it drops one nail and tilts. A single playing card (the nine of spades) slides off the table and falls face-up. Freeze on it. Fade to black.

rookie.title: The Banshee of Clonard Street
rookie.flavor: The rain hasn't stopped in three days. Inside the cramped terraced house on Clonard Street, Declan Maguire lies dead on the back-room floor, a single wound to the chest. The only witness — a frightened volunteer named Brigid — claims she heard a wailing outside the window just before the shot, and saw no living hand fire it. Someone in this safehouse planned a perfect crime and hid behind a ghost story.
rookie.emptyState: The banshee wails. The truth doesn't.

items.name: Seamus Riordan
items.detail: Senior IRA quartermaster who controlled the safehouse keys and resented Maguire's rising influence.

items.name: Brigid Callaghan
items.detail: Young volunteer and the sole witness, whose account of the evening shifts with every telling.

items.name: Colm Devlin
items.detail: A courier who delivered dispatches to the safehouse and was seen leaving minutes before the shot was heard.

items.name: The Back Room
items.detail: Where Maguire's body was found, accessible only through the kitchen corridor.

items.name: The Coal Cellar
items.detail: A low-ceilinged storage space beneath the house with a hatch to the rear alley.

items.name: The Kitchen
items.detail: The central room where volunteers gathered, smelling of stewed tea and cigarette smoke.

items.name: Service Revolver
items.detail: A .38 Webley kept in the quartermaster's locked strongbox — one round recently fired.

items.name: Sharpened Hurley Spike
items.detail: A length of hurley handle filed to a point, found hidden beneath the coal cellar steps.

items.name: Strangling Cord
items.detail: A length of electrical flex knotted at both ends, found stuffed behind the kitchen boiler.

clues.heading: Single Entry Wound
clues.text: The army doctor called to examine Maguire's body notes a single puncture wound to the left chest — consistent with a narrow, rigid implement driven upward. There is no gunshot residue on Maguire's clothing and no bullet recovered from the room.
clues.alone: This clue eliminates the revolver as the murder weapon, pointing instead to a bladed or spiked implement.

links.insight: The puncture wound angle combined with the cellar's low ceiling confirms the spike was driven upward from below, matching the coal cellar's hatch position directly beneath where Maguire fell.

clues.heading: The Wailing Story
clues.text: Brigid Callaghan tells the officer she was in the kitchen the entire evening and heard a banshee wail from the back yard just before Maguire cried out. She insists she never left the kitchen and saw no one enter the back room. Her hands, the officer notes, are ice-cold despite the coal fire burning nearby.
clues.alone: Brigid claims a continuous kitchen presence, which would make her the only person who could account for the back room entry.

links.insight: Brigid's ice-cold hands despite the burning kitchen fire directly contradict her claim of staying inside, indicating she was recently outdoors in the Belfast rain.

clues.heading: The Muddy Hatch
clues.text: The coal cellar hatch leading to the rear alley is found unlatched from the inside. A clear trail of damp clay footprints — small in size — leads from the hatch up the cellar steps and stops at the floorboards directly beneath the spot where Maguire's body lies.
clues.alone: The hatch was used from the alley side, and small feet tracked mud to the position beneath Maguire.

links.insight: The small footprint dimensions match Colm Devlin's boot size recorded in the quartermaster's log, proving Devlin re-entered through the cellar after appearing to leave.

clues.heading: The Spike Beneath the Steps
clues.text: The sharpened hurley spike is discovered wedged beneath the lowest cellar step, still bearing traces of blood along its tip. The angle of the blood smear runs from tip toward handle — consistent with an upward thrust through a gap in the floorboards.
clues.alone: The spike was thrust upward from the cellar, not carried into the back room — eliminating any above-floor attacker.

links.insight: The upward spike angle combined with the chest wound location proves the killer struck from below through the cellar, never entering the back room at all.

clues.heading: Rain on the Collar
clues.text: When Brigid removes her coat to be interviewed, the officer notices the back of her collar is damp — not from perspiration but from fresh rainfall. The rain had stopped forty minutes before the interview but had been falling heavily at the time of the murder.
clues.alone: Brigid was outside in the rain during the period she claims to have been continuously inside the kitchen.

links.insight: Brigid's wet collar directly exposes her banshee account as a fabrication designed to misdirect from her own absence from the kitchen during the murder window.

clues.heading: The Quartermaster's Boot Log
clues.text: Seamus Riordan keeps a meticulous logbook of every volunteer's kit issuance. The entry for Colm Devlin records a pair of size-six army-surplus boots issued three weeks prior — an unusually small size noted with a margin comment.
clues.alone: Devlin wears size-six boots, an uncommon small size among the male volunteers recorded in the log.

links.insight: Devlin's documented size-six boot matches the small cellar footprints precisely, confirming he is the figure who entered through the hatch and killed Maguire from below.

clues.heading: Devlin's Departure Witness
clues.text: Two volunteers confirm they watched Colm Devlin leave through the front door at approximately 9:40 pm — roughly ten minutes before the shot was heard. Both are certain he did not return through the front. Neither was watching the rear of the property.
clues.alone: Devlin's front-door departure is confirmed, but the rear of the property was unobserved, leaving the cellar hatch unaccounted for.

links.insight: Devlin's confirmed front exit paired with the rear alley's unobserved status reveals the cellar hatch as his re-entry route, completing a planned escape-and-return sequence.

clues.heading: The Alley Map
clues.text: A hand-drawn sketch found in Devlin's coat pocket shows the rear alley behind Clonard Street annotated with the cellar hatch position, a timing note reading '9 mins', and an arrow marking the back-room floorboard gap. The sketch is drawn in Devlin's own hand.
clues.alone: Devlin pre-planned access to the cellar hatch and knew exactly where to position himself beneath Maguire.

links.insight: The annotated timing note of nine minutes matches the gap between Devlin's witnessed departure and the murder, proving his exit and cellar re-entry were choreographed in advance.

rookie.epilogue: Colm Devlin had planned it for weeks. He knew Maguire's habit of standing at the back-room window each evening, and he had measured the gap in the floorboards himself on a supply run. He left through the front door in full view of two witnesses, slipped down the entry to the rear alley, lifted the unlatched hatch, and drove the sharpened spike upward through the boards — never entering the room, never leaving a trace above the floor. Brigid Callaghan was not his accomplice but a useful fool: her genuine panic at finding the body, and her local belief in banshee omens, gave her account just enough sincerity to muddy the water. Devlin had counted on the supernatural atmosphere of wartime Belfast to do the rest. Seamus Riordan was entirely uninvolved — his suspicion of Maguire was real, but it never crossed into violence. The perfect crime unraveled not through confession but through mud, boot size, and a wet collar.

detective.title: The Banshee of Clonard Street
detective.flavor: The rain hasn't stopped in three days. Inside the cramped terraced house on Clonard Street, Declan Maguire lies dead on the back-room floor, a single wound to the chest. The only witness — a frightened volunteer named Brigid — claims she heard wailing outside the window just before the shot, and saw no living hand fire it. The quartermaster is furious. The courier is gone. Someone in this safehouse planned a perfect crime and needs you to miss it.
detective.emptyState: The banshee wails. The truth doesn't.

items.name: Seamus Riordan
items.detail: Senior IRA quartermaster who controlled keys, logbooks, and resented Maguire's authority deeply.

items.name: Brigid Callaghan
items.detail: Young volunteer and sole witness whose banshee account contains internal contradictions and physical impossibilities.

items.name: Colm Devlin
items.detail: Courier with confirmed front-door departure, a hand-drawn approach map, and a typewriter that produced the informer file.

items.name: Father Peadar Og
items.detail: Local priest whose name appears twice in the logbook — once legitimately, once in a different ink and unsigned.

items.name: The Back Room
items.detail: Where Maguire's body was found — accessible only through the kitchen corridor, with a floorboard gap above the cellar.

items.name: The Coal Cellar
items.detail: Low-ceilinged storage beneath the house, hatch to the rear alley unlatched from inside.

items.name: The Kitchen
items.detail: Central room where Brigid claims she was stationed all evening beside the coal fire.

items.name: The Front Hallway
items.detail: Where volunteers were confirmed departing — observed only at the front, not the rear.

items.name: Service Revolver
items.detail: A .38 Webley, one round fired — but no bullet recovered and no gunshot residue on the victim.

items.name: Sharpened Hurley Spike
items.detail: Filed hurley handle found beneath the cellar steps, blood trace running tip to handle in an upward smear.

items.name: Strangling Cord
items.detail: Electrical flex found behind the kitchen boiler — no fibre trace on the victim's neck.

items.name: Poison Draft
items.detail: A small glass vial found empty in the coal cellar — contents unknown, label removed.

items.name: Informer Silencing
items.detail: A typed file names Maguire as an RUC contact — produced on Devlin's typewriter.

items.name: Personal Vendetta
items.detail: A recorded dispute between Devlin and Maguire over a woman from Derry, three years unresolved.

items.name: Inheritance of Command
items.detail: Maguire's death would elevate the next senior figure — a motive that benefits Riordan most directly.

items.name: British Handler's Order
items.detail: Evidence suggests someone in the safehouse was acting on instructions from a British intelligence contact.

clues.heading: Wound Profile
clues.text: The examining doctor finds a single puncture wound to the left chest, angled sharply upward. No gunshot residue is present. The wound channel is narrow and consistent with a rigid pointed implement. The poison vial found in the cellar is sent for analysis — preliminary results show no toxic residue inside it.
clues.alone: The wound profile eliminates the revolver and the poison vial, narrowing the weapon to a spiked implement driven from below.

links.insight: The upward wound angle combined with the cellar footprint trail confirms the killer attacked from below through the floorboard gap, never entering the back room.

clues.heading: Brigid's Shifting Account
clues.text: In her first statement Brigid says she heard the wail at 9:47. In her second, given an hour later, she places it at 9:51. The murder is fixed at approximately 9:50 by two volunteers who heard Maguire fall. She cannot explain the discrepancy and grows agitated when pressed.
clues.alone: Brigid's timeline inconsistency suggests she is either lying or concealing the true sequence of events she witnessed.

links.insight: Brigid's shifting timestamps combined with her damp collar prove she was outside during the murder window and constructed the banshee account to explain her absence.

clues.heading: Father Og's Visit Record
clues.text: The safehouse sign-in logbook, kept by Riordan, shows Father Peadar Og arrived at 3:15 pm and departed at 4:00 pm — a forty-five minute pastoral visit. However, a second entry in a different ink shows his name again at 9:30 pm, unsigned, as though added later.
clues.alone: The unsigned second entry for Father Og suggests someone falsified his presence in the log during the murder window.

links.insight: The forged log entry for Father Og during the murder window points to Devlin, who had access to the logbook during his dispatch delivery and needed to seed a false suspect.

clues.heading: The Annotated Sketch
clues.text: A hand-drawn sketch found in Devlin's coat pocket maps the rear alley, marks the cellar hatch, and annotates a nine-minute timing window with an arrow pointing to the floorboard gap beneath the back room. The sketch is in Devlin's handwriting. A margin note reads: 'motive — handled'.
clues.alone: Devlin pre-planned the attack route and timing in detail, and the note confirms he considered motive a solved problem.

links.insight: The sketch's 'motive handled' note paired with the informer file confirms Devlin knew Maguire was suspected as a tout and used that suspicion as his operational justification.

clues.heading: Cellar Approach Trail
clues.text: The coal cellar hatch is unlatched from the inside. A trail of small damp-clay footprints runs from the hatch up the steps and positions itself directly below the floorboard gap beneath the back room. The sharpened hurley spike rests against the lower step, blood trace running from tip to handle.
clues.alone: The spike was deployed from the cellar upward — the killer never entered the back room above.

links.insight: The cellar trail and spike position combined with the upward wound angle prove beyond doubt that the murder was committed from below through the floor, requiring cellar access only.

clues.heading: Rainfall Evidence
clues.text: Brigid's coat collar is damp with fresh rain. The kitchen fire has been burning for three hours. No window in the kitchen was open. The rain stopped forty minutes before the interview — meaning Brigid was outdoors during the killing, not beside the fire as she claims.
clues.alone: Brigid was outside in the rain during the murder, directly contradicting her statement of continuous kitchen presence.

links.insight: Brigid's wet collar combined with her inconsistent timestamps exposes the banshee story as a fabrication to cover her own unexplained outdoor absence during the killing.

clues.heading: The Informer File
clues.text: A folded sheet tucked inside the logbook — separate from Riordan's entries — lists Maguire's name alongside three dates and the notation 'RUC contact confirmed.' The document is unsigned but typed on the same portable typewriter found in Devlin's dispatch bag.
clues.alone: The informer file implicating Maguire was produced on Devlin's typewriter, suggesting Devlin authored or planted it.

links.insight: Devlin's typewriter producing the informer file alongside the forged log entry shows Devlin constructed both the motive narrative and the false suspect trail in advance.

clues.heading: The Handler Signal
clues.text: British Army signals intelligence recovered after the fact logs a short-wave transmission from the Clonard Street area at 9:20 pm — thirty minutes before the murder. The signal is a pre-arranged contact code associated with a courier asset. Devlin's courier role gave him the equipment and the access.
clues.alone: A British intelligence contact signal was broadcast from Devlin's known operating area thirty minutes before the murder.

links.insight: The pre-murder handler signal combined with the sketch note 'motive handled' confirms Devlin acted on British intelligence orders and framed the killing as an internal informer execution.

detective.epilogue: Colm Devlin had been a British intelligence asset for eleven months. His handler had identified Maguire — incorrectly, as it turned out — as a security liability, and ordered his removal in a way that would look like internal IRA justice. Devlin manufactured the informer file on his own typewriter, forged Father Og's logbook entry to seed confusion, and constructed the physical approach with the precision of a man who had rehearsed it. He left through the front door in full view of witnesses, dropped through the rear alley, and killed Maguire from below — never appearing in the room at all. Brigid Callaghan was neither accomplice nor conspirator; her banshee story was genuine superstition triggered by genuine panic, and her wet collar gave her away as a woman who had simply stepped outside for air at the worst possible moment. Seamus Riordan's resentment of Maguire was real but irrelevant — he was the intended fall if anyone pushed back. Father Peadar Og had no idea his name had been written into an evening he spent at home in Clonard. Devlin was lifted by British forces three days later — officially arrested, quietly resettled.

inspector.title: The Banshee of Clonard Street
inspector.flavor: The rain hasn't stopped in three days. Declan Maguire is dead on the back-room floor of the Clonard Street safehouse, a narrow wound punched upward through his chest. The only witness insists a banshee called his name. The courier is gone. The logbook has been touched. A signal was broadcast before the shot was fired. Someone constructed a perfect crime inside a house full of paranoid men — and they almost got away with it.
inspector.emptyState: The banshee wails. The truth doesn't.

items.name: Seamus Riordan
items.detail: Senior quartermaster who controlled keys, logbooks, and resented Maguire's authority deeply.

items.name: Brigid Callaghan
items.detail: Sole witness whose banshee account contains internal contradictions and physical impossibilities.

items.name: Colm Devlin
items.detail: Courier with confirmed front-door departure, a hand-drawn approach map, and a typewriter that produced the informer file.

items.name: Father Peadar Og
items.detail: Local priest whose name appears twice in the logbook — once legitimately, once in a different ink and unsigned.

items.name: The Back Room
items.detail: Where Maguire's body was found — accessible only through the kitchen corridor, with a floorboard gap above the cellar.

items.name: The Coal Cellar
items.detail: Low-ceilinged storage beneath the house, hatch to the rear alley unlatched from inside.

items.name: The Kitchen
items.detail: Central room where Brigid claims she was stationed all evening beside the coal fire.

items.name: The Front Hallway
items.detail: Where volunteers were confirmed departing — observed only at the front, not the rear.

items.name: The Rear Alley
items.detail: The unlit entry behind Clonard Street, unobserved by any volunteer, leading directly to the cellar hatch.

items.name: Service Revolver
items.detail: A .38 Webley, one round fired — but no bullet recovered and no gunshot residue on the victim.

items.name: Sharpened Hurley Spike
items.detail: Filed hurley handle found beneath the cellar steps, blood trace running tip to handle in an upward smear.

items.name: Strangling Cord
items.detail: Electrical flex found behind the kitchen boiler — no fibre trace on the victim's neck.

items.name: Poison Draft
items.detail: Empty vial in the cellar — laboratory analysis returns no toxic residue, ruling it out.

items.name: Shiv from Logbook Binding
items.detail: A thin metal binding strip from an older logbook sharpened to a point — found wedged in the cellar wall, clean.

items.name: Informer Silencing
items.detail: A typed file names Maguire as an RUC contact — produced on Devlin's typewriter.

items.name: Personal Vendetta
items.detail: A recorded dispute between Devlin and Maguire over a woman from Derry, three years unresolved.

items.name: Inheritance of Command
items.detail: Maguire's death would elevate the next senior figure — a motive that benefits Riordan most directly.

items.name: British Handler's Order
items.detail: A signals intercept logs a pre-arranged British intelligence contact code from Devlin's area thirty minutes before the murder.

items.name: Brigid Callaghan
items.detail: The witness whose story, true or false, created the confusion window Devlin needed.

items.name: Father Peadar Og
items.detail: The priest whose afternoon visit gave Devlin a cover name to forge into the evening logbook.

items.name: British Handler (unnamed)
items.detail: The intelligence contact who ordered the hit and arranged Devlin's extraction three days later.

items.name: Seamus Riordan
items.detail: The quartermaster who may have left the cellar hatch unlatched — whether knowingly or not is unresolved.

items.name: None — Solo Operation
items.detail: All evidence of accomplice involvement may be coincidental; Devlin may have acted entirely alone.

clues.heading: Compound Wound Analysis
clues.text: The pathologist's full report notes the puncture wound is 11mm wide, angled at 34 degrees upward, and shows no secondary bruising consistent with a hand-to-hand struggle. The channel depth reaches the pericardium. The revolver, strangling cord, poison vial, and logbook shiv are all eliminated by wound profile alone. Only the hurley spike's dimensions and blood-smear direction are consistent.
clues.alone: All weapons except the sharpened hurley spike are eliminated by the wound's geometry and the absence of supporting physical evidence.

links.insight: The spike's wound geometry combined with the cellar footprint terminus beneath the floorboard gap proves the killer struck from below, requiring only cellar access and never entering the back room.

clues.heading: Three-Version Timeline
clues.text: Brigid gives three accounts across two interviews. Version one: wail at 9:47, she was at the fire. Version two: wail at 9:51, she had briefly stepped to the window. Version three, extracted under patient questioning: she cannot place herself for approximately eight minutes between 9:43 and 9:51. Each version is internally consistent — but the three together are not.
clues.alone: Brigid's three accounts reveal an eight-minute gap she cannot account for, despite her insistence she never left the kitchen.

links.insight: Brigid's unaccounted eight-minute gap precisely overlaps the rear alley rainfall window proven by her wet collar, revealing she was outside during the murder and witnessed something she will not name.

clues.heading: Logbook Ink Discrepancy
clues.text: Forensic examination of Riordan's logbook under UV light reveals two entries in different inks: Father Og's afternoon visit at 3:15 in blue ballpoint, and a second unsigned entry at 9:30 in black ballpoint. The blue pen is in Riordan's desk drawer. The black pen is not found in the safehouse. Devlin's dispatch bag contains a black ballpoint of the same brand.
clues.alone: The forged 9:30 logbook entry for Father Og was written with a black pen matching one in Devlin's possession.

links.insight: Devlin's black pen forging Father Og's evening logbook entry combined with his typewriter producing the informer file confirms a coordinated pre-murder document campaign designed to seed false suspects.

clues.heading: Signals Intercept Full Record
clues.text: The full SIGINT record for the Clonard Street area that evening shows two transmissions: one at 9:20 pm (pre-arranged contact code) and a second at 10:15 pm (extraction confirmation code). Both codes are associated with the same courier asset. Devlin's post-arrest debrief, declassified in 1994, confirms he transmitted both.
clues.alone: Devlin transmitted a pre-murder contact signal and a post-murder extraction confirmation — both attributed to him in declassified records.

links.insight: The two transmissions bracketing the murder, combined with Devlin's confirmed extraction three days later, establish that British intelligence coordinated the killing and Devlin's removal was pre-arranged.

clues.heading: Cellar Geometry Report
clues.text: A surveyor's sketch made the following morning maps the cellar exactly. The hatch entry point, the footprint trail terminus, and the floorboard gap form a straight line. The gap is 14cm wide — sufficient for the hurley spike. A chalk mark on the cellar ceiling directly below the gap suggests the position was pre-marked. The chalk is fresh.
clues.alone: The cellar was pre-marked for the strike position, confirming advance reconnaissance and deliberate preparation.

links.insight: The pre-marked cellar strike position combined with the spike wound geometry confirms a rehearsed, precision attack from below — not an improvised act, but a planned execution.

clues.heading: Precipitation and Collar Evidence
clues.text: The Met Office log for west Belfast records continuous rain from 8:10 pm to 9:48 pm. Brigid's coat collar is saturated on the outside and partially dried — consistent with exposure at the height of rainfall followed by approximately twenty minutes indoors. The kitchen fire was burning. No kitchen window was open. The saturation cannot be explained by indoor sources.
clues.alone: Brigid was outdoors in the rain between approximately 9:20 and 9:48 — the precise window covering the murder.

links.insight: Brigid's collar saturation during the exact murder rainfall window fills her unaccounted eight minutes and proves she was in the rear alley or vicinity when Devlin passed through — making her an inadvertent witness, not a willing one.

clues.heading: Typewriter Comparison
clues.text: The informer file naming Maguire as an RUC contact is typed on paper with a distinctive serif 'e' key misalignment — the same misalignment found in three legitimate dispatch documents typed by Devlin and verified by the quartermaster's records. The file was produced on Devlin's machine. The content is fabricated: post-conflict review found no evidence Maguire was ever an informer.
clues.alone: Devlin fabricated the informer file on his own typewriter, creating a false motive for Maguire's killing that pointed blame at IRA leadership.

links.insight: Devlin's forged informer file combined with his forged logbook entry for Father Og forms a two-document disinformation strategy designed to distribute suspicion and prevent any single person from being identified as the killer.

clues.heading: The 1994 Declassification
clues.text: A British intelligence summary declassified in 1994 confirms that Devlin was extracted to England three days after the Clonard Street killing, resettled under a new identity, and debriefed over six weeks. The summary notes: 'Asset confirms solo execution. No IRA-side accomplice recruited or used. Witness (female, volunteer) observed asset in alley but did not identify. No action taken.' Brigid saw him. She chose silence.
clues.alone: British intelligence confirms Devlin acted alone and that Brigid Callaghan observed him in the alley but did not report it.

links.insight: The declassified confirmation of Devlin's solo operation combined with both signals transmissions establishes the complete chain: British handler ordered the killing, Devlin executed it alone, and his extraction was pre-arranged before the shot was fired.

inspector.epilogue: Colm Devlin had been run by British intelligence for nearly a year when the order came. His handler had misidentified Maguire as a security risk — a mistake that would not be acknowledged for decades — and instructed Devlin to execute him in a way that would read as internal IRA justice. Devlin was thorough. He fabricated the informer file on his own typewriter, forged Father Og's name into the evening logbook to seed a false trail, pre-marked the cellar ceiling with chalk, and rehearsed the approach from the rear alley at least once. He left through the front door in full view of two men, slipped down the entry, lifted the hatch he had left unlatched on an earlier visit, and drove the hurley spike upward through the floorboard gap — a single thrust, measured in advance. He was in the cellar for less than four minutes. Brigid Callaghan had stepped into the rear alley for air. She saw Devlin emerge from the hatch, recognized him, and understood immediately what had happened. She said nothing — not to protect him, but because in Belfast in 1972, what you did not say was the only thing that kept you alive. Her banshee story was not a lie so much as the only version of events she could afford to tell. Seamus Riordan was innocent of everything except a ledger full of grievances he never acted on. Father Peadar Og never knew his name had been written into an evening he spent at home in Clonard. Devlin was in England by the weekend. The 1994 declassification gave Brigid Callaghan her answer twenty-two years late: the man she saw in the alley had been a British agent all along, and the movement had buried one of its own on a file a courier typed himself.
